description: Exploratory prototype for privacy-preserving DeFi execution on public blockchains. Transactions look like normal ETH transfers using stealth addresses + encrypted instructions + bonded relayers + ZK proofs. Approach A: single optimistic relayer with Groth16 proof of correct execution. Local Foundry/Anvil only.

userStories.title: Project scaffold — Foundry + Rust + Circom
userStories.description: As a developer, I need a monorepo with Foundry (Solidity), Rust (cargo workspace), and Circom directories so all components can be built from one place.
acceptanceCriteria: contracts/ directory with Foundry project (foundry.toml, src/, test/, script/) — forge build passes
relayer/ directory with Rust cargo workspace containing two crates: stealth-crypto (lib) and relayer (bin)
circuits/ directory with a trivial test circuit (e.g., signal constraint) that compiles with circom
package.json at root with snarkjs and circomlib as devDependencies
forge build passes with zero errors
cargo build passes with zero errors
Circom test circuit compiles to R1CS + WASM via circom compiler
userStories.notes: Use forge init for contracts. Use cargo new --lib for stealth-crypto and cargo new for relayer. npm init + npm install snarkjs circomlib for circuits tooling.

userStories.title: Stealth address library (Rust)
userStories.description: As a developer, I need a Rust library that generates stealth addresses using ECDH on secp256k1, so users can send funds to unlinkable one-time addresses that only the relayer can spend.
acceptanceCriteria: stealth-crypto crate with StealthAddress module
RelayerKeys struct: spend keypair (privkey + pubkey) + view keypair (privkey + pubkey) — the meta-address
User generates ephemeral keypair, computes ECDH shared secret with relayer's view pubkey
Stealth address derived: ethereum address of (hash(shared_secret) * G + relayer_spend_pubkey)
Relayer computes stealth private key: hash(shared_secret) + relayer_spend_privkey
Function to check if a tx was sent to a relayer's stealth address given the ephemeral pubkey
5+ unit tests: key generation, shared secret agreement, stealth derivation, private key recovery, deterministic output for fixed seed
cargo test passes
userStories.notes: Use k256 crate for secp256k1 ECDH. Use tiny-keccak for hashing. Ethereum address = last 20 bytes of keccak256(uncompressed_pubkey[1:]). The ephemeral pubkey R is what gets embedded in the transaction data field.

userStories.title: Instruction encryption (Rust)
userStories.description: As a developer, I need to encrypt DeFi instructions with the ECDH shared secret so only the intended relayer can read them.
acceptanceCriteria: Instruction struct: action_type (u8), token_in ([u8;20]), token_out ([u8;20]), amount_in (U256), min_amount_out (U256), recipient ([u8;20]), deadline (u64), nonce (u64)
Deterministic serialization to bytes (big-endian, fixed-width fields)
Encryption key derived from shared secret via HKDF-SHA256 with info='stealth-defi-encryption'
AES-256-GCM encryption with random 12-byte nonce, returns nonce || ciphertext || tag
Decrypt function recovers original Instruction struct
commitment() function returns keccak256(serialized_instruction)
Unit tests: round-trip, wrong key fails, tampered ciphertext fails (GCM auth), commitment deterministic, different nonces produce different ciphertexts
cargo test passes
userStories.notes: Use aes-gcm crate for encryption, hkdf + sha2 for key derivation. The nonce must be prepended to ciphertext so the receiver knows it. U256 can use ethers-core or alloy-primitives.

userStories.title: Commitment and bond contract (Solidity)
userStories.description: As a relayer, I need a smart contract where I post a bond alongside my execution commitment, so users have economic guarantees of correct execution.
acceptanceCriteria: StealthEscrow.sol with postCommitment(bytes32 commitment) payable — relayer sends ETH bond
Commitment struct stored: commitment hash, bond amount (msg.value), relayer address, challengeDeadline (block.timestamp + 7 days), zkpVerified (bool), released (bool)
releaseBond(uint256 id) — callable by relayer after challengeDeadline, transfers bond back
challenge(uint256 id) — placeholder for challenge logic (just emits event for now)
recoverFunds(uint256 id) payable — user recovery after 14-day timeout if relayer disappeared
Minimum bond enforced (configurable, default 1 ether)
Events: CommitmentPosted, BondReleased, ChallengeSubmitted, FundsRecovered
Foundry tests: post commitment, release after deadline (warp time), reject early release, recovery after timeout, reject low bond
forge test passes
userStories.notes: Use forge-std for testing (vm.warp, vm.prank, vm.deal). Challenge mechanism is a placeholder — full implementation comes with ZKP integration in US-008.

userStories.title: Mock AMM contract (Solidity)
userStories.description: As a developer, I need a simple AMM pool for testing swaps locally since we can't fork mainnet Uniswap.
acceptanceCriteria: MockERC20.sol — minimal ERC20 with public mint(address to, uint256 amount) function
MockAMM.sol — constant-product AMM (x * y = k) with two tokens set at construction
addLiquidity(uint256 amountA, uint256 amountB) — transfers both tokens from sender, mints LP tracking
swap(address tokenIn, uint256 amountIn, uint256 minAmountOut) — constant product swap with slippage check
getAmountOut(address tokenIn, uint256 amountIn) view — preview swap output
Deploy script: creates TokenA, TokenB, AMM, mints tokens to deployer, adds initial liquidity
Foundry tests: add liquidity, swap A to B, swap B to A, slippage revert, zero amount revert
forge test passes
userStories.notes: Keep it dead simple — no fees, no LP tokens as ERC20, no flash loans. This is just a test fixture. Use OpenZeppelin ERC20 as base for MockERC20 if available, otherwise implement minimal ERC20.

userStories.title: Circom circuit — instruction commitment proof
userStories.description: As a developer, I need a ZK circuit that proves an instruction matches a public commitment hash without revealing the instruction content.
acceptanceCriteria: circuits/instruction_commitment.circom using circomlib's Poseidon hasher
Private inputs: action_type, token_in, token_out, amount_in, min_amount_out, recipient, deadline, nonce (each as field element)
Public input: commitment_hash
Constraint: Poseidon(action_type, token_in, token_out, amount_in, min_amount_out, recipient, deadline, nonce) === commitment_hash
Circuit compiles with circom, R1CS and WASM generated
scripts/compile.sh compiles the circuit
scripts/test_witness.sh generates a witness with known test inputs and verifies it
Constraint count logged to stdout
userStories.notes: Poseidon with 8 inputs may need to be split into multiple rounds depending on circomlib's Poseidon implementation (which supports up to ~16 inputs). Use the Poseidon hash from circomlib. Field elements are BN128 scalar field (< ~2^254).

userStories.title: Circom circuit — execution correctness proof
userStories.description: As a developer, I need a ZK circuit that proves the relayer executed correctly (right recipient, sufficient output, within deadline) without revealing instruction details.
acceptanceCriteria: circuits/execution_proof.circom — includes instruction commitment as sub-circuit
Additional private inputs: execution_amount_out, execution_timestamp
Additional public inputs: expected_recipient, min_expected_amount, max_deadline

Constraints: (1) instruction commitment matches public hash, (2) recipient from instruction == expected_recipient, (3) execution_amount_out >= min_amount_out from instruction, (4) execution_timestamp <= deadline from instruction
Groth16 trusted setup: powers of tau ceremony (BN128, sufficient for circuit size)
Proving key and verification key generated
Solidity verifier contract auto-generated via snarkjs export solidityverifier
Test: generate proof with valid inputs, verify with snarkjs verify — passes
Test: attempt proof with invalid inputs (wrong recipient) — witness generation fails
userStories.notes: For powers of tau, use snarkjs powersoftau new bn128 16 (2^16 = 65536 constraints should be enough). The comparators (LessEqThan, IsEqual) come from circomlib. Solidity verifier goes into contracts/src/Groth16Verifier.sol.

userStories.title: On-chain ZKP verifier integration (Solidity)
userStories.description: As a relayer, I want to submit a ZK proof to skip the 7-day challenge period and get my bond released immediately.
acceptanceCriteria: Groth16Verifier.sol from snarkjs export placed in contracts/src/
StealthEscrow.sol updated with verifyAndRelease(uint256 commitmentId, uint256[2] memory a, uint256[2][2] memory b, uint256[2] memory c, uint256[] memory publicInputs)
verifyAndRelease calls Groth16Verifier.verifyProof — if valid, sets zkpVerified=true and releases bond immediately
If proof invalid — reverts, bond stays locked
Foundry test with a real Groth16 proof (generated from circuit in US-007): submit proof, verify bond released
Foundry test: submit garbage proof, verify revert
forge test passes
userStories.notes: The tricky part is getting a real proof into Foundry tests. Options: (1) hardcode proof values from a snarkjs run, (2) use ffi cheatcode to call snarkjs from Foundry test. Option 1 is simpler for a prototype.

userStories.title: Rust relayer — Anvil monitoring and stealth detection
userStories.description: As a relayer, I need to watch the local chain for incoming stealth transfers, detect them using my view key, and decrypt the embedded instructions.
acceptanceCriteria: relayer binary connects to local Anvil node via JSON-RPC (ethers-rs or alloy)
Polls new blocks (or subscribes to newHeads) for ETH transfers
For each transfer: checks if calldata starts with 33-byte compressed ephemeral pubkey
Computes ECDH shared secret, derives expected stealth address, checks against tx recipient
On match: decrypts instruction from remaining calldata using shared secret
Logs: matched stealth transfer detected, decoded instruction fields
Handles gracefully: no matching txs, malformed calldata, RPC connection errors
Integration test on Anvil: deploy nothing, just send raw ETH tx with stealth payload, verify relayer detects it
cargo test passes
userStories.notes: Use ethers-rs Provider with HTTP transport for Anvil. Anvil default URL: http://127.0.0.1:8545. The relayer needs its own keypair loaded from env or config file. For the integration test, start Anvil as a child process or assume it's running.

userStories.title: Rust relayer — execute swap and post proof
userStories.description: As a relayer, after detecting a stealth instruction, I need to execute the swap on the mock AMM and post a ZK proof to the escrow contract.
acceptanceCriteria: Relayer derives stealth private key, creates wallet from it
Sends tx from stealth address to transfer received ETH (or wrap/swap as needed by instruction)
Calls MockAMM.swap() with instruction parameters (token_in, amount_in, min_amount_out)
Transfers swapped tokens to instruction.recipient
Computes instruction commitment (keccak256)
Calls StealthEscrow.postCommitment() with bond
Generates Groth16 proof by shelling out to snarkjs (or using ark-circom if feasible)
Calls StealthEscrow.verifyAndRelease() with the proof
All steps logged with tx hashes
Integration test on Anvil with deployed contracts: full flow from detection to bond release
cargo test passes
userStories.notes: For ZKP generation, shelling out to snarkjs via Command::new('node') is the simplest approach. Write input.json, run snarkjs groth16 fullprove, read proof.json. The relayer needs ABI bindings for StealthEscrow and MockAMM — generate with forge bind or manually define.

userStories.title: End-to-end integration test
userStories.description: As a developer, I need a single script that runs the complete protocol flow on local Anvil to prove the system works.
acceptanceCriteria: Shell script (scripts/e2e.sh) or Rust integration test that runs the full flow:
1. Starts Anvil (or connects to running instance on 8545)
2. Deploys: MockERC20 x2, MockAMM (with liquidity), StealthEscrow, Groth16Verifier
3. Generates relayer keypairs and user ephemeral keypair
4. User derives stealth address, encrypts swap instruction, sends ETH + encrypted data
5. Relayer detects stealth transfer, decrypts instruction
6. Relayer executes swap on MockAMM, sends tokens to recipient
7. Relayer posts commitment + bond, generates ZKP, submits proof
8. Asserts: bond released, recipient received tokens, events emitted correctly
Prints summary: gas cost per step, total gas, proof generation time
Exit code 0 on success, non-zero on any assertion failure
userStories.notes: This is the money shot for the dissertation. If this passes, the protocol works. A shell script orchestrating forge script + cargo run is fine. Alternatively, a single Rust binary that does everything is cleaner.

userStories.title: Privacy analysis
userStories.description: As a researcher, I need to analyze the on-chain footprint to evaluate whether stealth transfers are distinguishable from normal ETH transfers.
acceptanceCriteria: Script (Python or Rust) that reads on-chain state from Anvil after e2e test
Analysis 1 — Linkability: examines all txs, checks if sender address appears linked to final recipient in any on-chain data
Analysis 2 — Distinguishability: compares stealth transfer (gas, calldata size, value) vs a normal ETH transfer baseline
Analysis 3 — Relayer footprint: checks if relayer's execution txs are linkable to original deposit by timing, value, or address reuse
Comparison table: gas overhead of stealth swap vs direct AMM swap
Output: analysis/privacy-report.md with findings and metrics table
Script runs without errors
userStories.notes: Use cast or eth_getBlock/eth_getTransaction RPC calls to pull tx data from Anvil. The analysis doesn't need to be sophisticated — basic heuristics are fine for a prototype. The key finding to prove: no deterministic on-chain link between deposit tx and swap output.

userStories.title: Benchmark suite
userStories.description: As a researcher, I need quantitative benchmarks for the dissertation's evaluation chapter.
acceptanceCriteria: Script that measures: gas cost per operation (stealth transfer, commitment, proof verification, bond release)
ZKP circuit constraint count
Proof generation time (average of 10 runs)
Proof size in bytes
End-to-end latency from deposit tx to bond release
Gas overhead: stealth swap total gas vs direct MockAMM.swap() gas (percentage)
Output: benchmarks/results.csv with raw numbers
Output: benchmarks/results.md with formatted comparison table
All measurements automated, no manual steps
Script runs without errors
userStories.notes: Run proof generation 10 times and take average + stddev. Gas costs can be pulled from tx receipts. For comparison, deploy a second scenario where user just calls MockAMM.swap() directly and measure that gas.
